representative.name: Stephen Lynch
representative.title: U.S. Representative
representative.office: 2109 Rayburn House Office Building, Washington, DC 20515
representative.background: Stephen Lynch is the U.S. Representative for Massachusetts' 8th Congressional District, which includes Quincy, South Boston, parts of Dorchester, Brockton, Plymouth, and communities on the South Shore. First elected in 2001, he is one of the most senior members of the Massachusetts delegation.
representative.backgroundFull:
Stephen Lynch is the U.S. Representative for Massachusetts' 8th Congressional District, representing Quincy, South Boston, Dorchester (part), Brockton, Plymouth, and communities along the South Shore and Cape Cod. First elected in 2001 in a special election, he is the most senior member of the Massachusetts congressional delegation and one of the few Democrats in Congress who regularly breaks with his party on certain votes.

Lynch was born in South Boston to a working-class Irish-American family and worked as a structural ironworker before attending Wentworth Institute of Technology for engineering. He later earned a J.D. from Boston College Law School and an M.A. from Harvard's Kennedy School. He served in the Massachusetts House and Senate before coming to Congress.

Lynch is known for his moderate-to-conservative positions on some social issues — he was one of the few House Democrats to oppose the Affordable Care Act in 2010 — while being strongly pro-labor and pro-worker economically. He is a member of the House Financial Services Committee and the House Oversight and Reform Committee, where he has been an active investigator of financial industry misconduct and federal contracting waste.

His office provides assistance with federal agencies including Social Security, Medicare, the VA, USCIS (immigration), and federal small business programs. He has been a consistent advocate for South Shore transportation, supporting the Red Line and commuter rail, and has worked on flood mitigation funding for the district's coastal communities.

Lynch serves on the Congressional Steel Caucus and the Congressional Shipbuilding Caucus, reflecting the industrial and maritime heritage of his district.

sources.label: Biographical Directory of Congress — Rep. Lynch

representative.name: Bruce J. Ayers
representative.title: State Representative
representative.background: Bruce J. Ayers is the Massachusetts State Representative for the 1st Norfolk District, representing portions of Quincy (Ward 3 Precincts 3–5, Ward 4 Precincts 1 & 3, and Ward 6) and parts of Randolph. First elected in 1999, he is one of the longest-serving legislators representing Quincy and has been a consistent champion for people with disabilities, small businesses, and senior services.
representative.backgroundFull:
Bruce J. Ayers is the Massachusetts State Representative for the 1st Norfolk District, covering Ward 3 Precincts 3, 4, and 5, Ward 4 Precincts 1 and 3, and Ward 6 of Quincy, as well as Precincts 7, 8, 11, and 12 of Randolph. He has represented this district since 1999, making him one of the longest-serving state legislators from Quincy.

Ayers was born on April 17, 1962, in Boston and grew up in Quincy, graduating from North Quincy High School. He attended Northeastern University and the Harvard University Extension School, and completed the Boston College Small Business Development Program. He owns Ayers Handicap Conversion Center, a business he has operated for over 35 years that retrofits vehicles with adaptive driving equipment for people with physical disabilities. This professional background has made disability rights and accessibility one of the cornerstones of his legislative work.

Before his election to the State House, Ayers served on the Quincy Democratic City Committee from 1990 to 2000 and was elected to the Quincy City Council for Ward 6 in 1992, serving there until his election to the legislature in 1999.

In the legislature, Ayers has been a steady advocate for the physically disabled, filing legislation to strengthen enforcement against illegal use of handicapped parking spaces. He has also worked on animal welfare legislation, senior services, and small business support. He hosts a regular television program, Legislative Update with Rep. Bruce Ayers, on Quincy public access TV, where he discusses legislation and interviews local officials — a reflection of his focus on constituent communication and transparency.

Note: Quincy is served by three State Representatives. Bruce Ayers covers the 1st Norfolk (Ward 3 Precincts 3–5, Ward 4 Precincts 1 & 3, Ward 6), Tackey Chan covers the 2nd Norfolk, and Ronald Mariano (House Speaker) covers the 3rd Norfolk. Residents should confirm their district at malegislature.gov/Search/FindMyLegislator.

Constituents can reach Rep. Ayers at Bruce.Ayers@mahouse.gov or (617) 722-2220.

sources.label: MA Legislature — Rep. Ayers Official Profile
City of Quincy — State Delegation Page

senator.name: John F. Keenan
senator.title: State Senator
senator.office: State House, Room 413-F, 24 Beacon Street, Boston, MA 02133
senator.background: John F. Keenan is the Massachusetts State Senator for the Norfolk and Plymouth District, representing Quincy, Abington, Braintree, Hanover, Holbrook, and Rockland. First elected in 2010, he is a Harvard graduate and Suffolk Law alumnus who previously served on the Quincy City Council. His legislative work focuses on public and behavioral health, election law, and housing.
senator.backgroundFull:
John F. Keenan has represented the Norfolk and Plymouth District in the Massachusetts State Senate since 2011, serving Quincy, Abington, Braintree, Hanover, Holbrook, and Rockland.

The fourth of seven children, John was raised in Quincy and attended Quincy Public Schools, graduating from North Quincy High School in 1982. He earned his way through Harvard University working in the athletic department equipment room, graduating with honors in 1986 with a concentration in government. He graduated from Suffolk University Law School in 1989, and in 2019 earned a Master's in Public Administration from the Harvard Kennedy School.

After law school, Keenan worked as a litigator, public defender, and attorney combating insurance fraud. He is admitted to practice in all Massachusetts State Courts, the Federal District Court, the Federal First Circuit, and the United States Supreme Court.

Before his Senate election, Keenan chaired the Quincy Mayor's Commission on Handicapped Affairs and served on the Quincy Zoning Board of Appeals for seven years. From 1997–2001 he was Chief of Staff/Executive Secretary under Quincy Mayor Jim Sheets, managing 22 municipal departments. From 2001–2010 he served as Executive Director of the Norfolk County Retirement System. He was appointed to the Quincy City Council in 2003, elected Councillor-at-Large in 2004, and chaired its Finance Committee for eight years.

Elected to the State Senate in 2010, Keenan currently serves as Senate Chair of the Joint Committee on Election Laws. He has also chaired the Joint Committees on Mental Health and Substance Abuse, Public Health, and Housing, and the Senate Committees on Bonding, Capital Expenditures, Post Audit and Oversight, and Intergovernmental Affairs.

John and his wife Jeanne Hopkins reside in Quincy and are the parents of three sons. He is a member of the Ward One Democratic Committee, Quincy Democratic City Committee, and the Harvard Club of Quincy. He coached in the Sacred Heart Youth Basketball Program and enjoys travel and photography.

Note: This profile is informational. It does not represent an endorsement.

mayor.name: Thomas Koch
mayor.title: Mayor
mayor.background: Thomas P. Koch is the 33rd Mayor of Quincy, Massachusetts, and the longest-serving mayor in the city's history. First sworn in on January 7, 2008, he is currently serving his seventh term. Koch was born and raised in Quincy and has dedicated his career entirely to public service in his hometown.
mayor.backgroundFull:
Thomas P. Koch is the 33rd Mayor of Quincy, Massachusetts, and the longest-serving mayor in the city's history. Born and raised in Quincy as the sixth of seven children of Richard and Simone Koch, he graduated from North Quincy High School in 1981 (class president) and spent approximately 20 years in various leadership positions within city government — including as Executive Director of the Parks and Forestry Department — before launching a successful campaign for mayor in 2007.

First sworn in on January 7, 2008, Koch has been re-elected six times. His administration has overseen a dramatic transformation of Quincy: the construction of three new state-of-the-art school buildings, replacement of aging water and sewer infrastructure, construction of seawalls and flood mitigation systems in response to major flooding events in 2010 and 2018, and a wholesale revitalization of downtown Quincy featuring new residential developments, restaurants, and nationally recognized public spaces including the Hancock-Adams Common.

Koch has also expanded Quincy's fire department to four ladder companies for the first time since 1989 and has pursued major road and sidewalk improvements citywide. Under his tenure, Quincy's population has grown from approximately 92,000 to over 100,000, and the city has earned recognition from publications including Boston Magazine, the Wall Street Journal, and the New York Times. Quincy has received bond rating upgrades from Wall Street during his tenure.

Koch's administration has not been without controversy. In 2024, his proposal to raise his salary from $159,000 to $285,000 drew public backlash and was deferred. In 2025, his unilateral decision to commission $850,000 in religious statues for the city's new public safety building prompted litigation and a state court injunction. He is a family man who married his high school sweetheart Christine Keenan in 1990; they have three children and live near his childhood home in North Quincy.

sources.label: City of Quincy — Mayor's Official Bio
WBUR: Koch in 7th term, quelling controversies (Nov 2025)
Koch for Quincy — Meet the Mayor

cityCouncil.name: Quincy City Council
cityCouncil.title: City Council
cityCouncil.background: The Quincy City Council is the legislative body of Quincy, Massachusetts, consisting of 9 members — 6 ward councilors representing each of Quincy's 6 wards, and 3 councilors-at-large. The Council works alongside Mayor Thomas Koch to set city policy, adopt the annual budget, and enact local ordinances.
cityCouncil.backgroundFull:
The Quincy City Council is the legislative body of Quincy, Massachusetts. It consists of 9 members: 6 ward councilors (one for each of Quincy's six wards — Ward 1 through Ward 6) and 3 councilors-at-large who represent the entire city. All members are elected to two-year terms.

The City Council works alongside Mayor Thomas Koch to govern Quincy. The Council's responsibilities include adopting the annual municipal budget, enacting local ordinances and zoning changes, approving major contracts and expenditures, and confirming certain mayoral appointments. The Council also holds regular public hearings on zoning variance requests and development projects.

Quincy's six wards cover distinct geographic areas of the city: Ward 1 (North Quincy, Wollaston), Ward 2 (Quincy Center, Merrymount), Ward 3 (Quincy Point, Marina Bay area), Ward 4 (West Quincy, Squantum), Ward 5 (South Quincy, Germantown), and Ward 6 (Montclair, Adams Shore, Atlantic area).

City Council meetings are held at Quincy City Hall, 1305 Hancock Street, and are open to the public. Residents may address the Council during public comment periods. To contact your ward councilor or an at-large councilor, visit the City of Quincy website at quincyma.gov or call City Hall at 617-376-1000.

additionalLocals.name: Ronald J. Mariano
additionalLocals.title: State Representative & Speaker of the House
additionalLocals.office: Speaker of the Massachusetts House of Representatives
additionalLocals.background: Ronald J. Mariano is the Speaker of the Massachusetts House of Representatives and State Representative for the 3rd Norfolk District, representing portions of Quincy, Weymouth, and Holbrook. A lifelong Quincy Point resident, he has served in the House since 1991 and was elected Speaker in December 2020. He was a key architect of Massachusetts' landmark 2006 health care reform law that served as a model for the Affordable Care Act.
additionalLocals.backgroundFull:
Ronald J. Mariano is the Speaker of the Massachusetts House of Representatives — the most powerful position in the state legislature — and the State Representative for the 3rd Norfolk District, covering Ward 2, Ward 4 Precinct 5, and Ward 5 Precinct 1A of Quincy, along with portions of Weymouth and Holbrook. He has served in the Massachusetts House since 1991 and was elected Speaker in December 2020, re-elected to the Speakership in January 2023 and January 2025.

Mariano was born in 1946 in Quincy and is a lifelong resident of Quincy Point. He attended Quincy Public Schools, earned a B.S. from Northeastern University, and an M.Ed. from the University of Massachusetts Boston. He taught in the Quincy Public Schools for 12 years and served on the Quincy School Committee for 18 years before his election to the House in 1991.

Over more than three decades in the legislature, Mariano rose through the ranks from backbencher to one of the most consequential figures in Massachusetts government. He served as Assistant Majority Leader and then Majority Leader from 2011 until his elevation to Speaker. As Speaker he oversees all House operations, sets the legislative agenda, appoints committee chairs, and controls the flow of legislation through the chamber.

Mariano's most significant legislative achievement is his role as House Chair of the Joint Committee on Financial Services and chief architect of the Massachusetts health care reform law of 2006 — the first in the nation to achieve near-universal coverage, and the model upon which the federal Affordable Care Act was based. President Obama has publicly credited Massachusetts' law as the template for the ACA. Mariano was also the lead House negotiator on the 2014 gun safety reform and the subsequent ban on bump stocks, making Massachusetts a national leader on firearms regulation.

As Speaker, Mariano oversaw the disbursement of more than $5 billion in American Rescue Plan Act (ARPA) funds to Massachusetts workers, businesses, and institutions during and after the COVID-19 pandemic. He has championed affordable housing legislation, life sciences investment, clean energy, and LGBTQ+ and reproductive rights.

Quincy residents in the 3rd Norfolk should contact the Speaker's office at Ronald.Mariano@mahouse.gov or (617) 722-2500. All Quincy residents benefit from having the Speaker of the House as one of their three state representatives — a position of enormous influence over the state budget and legislative priorities.

City of Quincy — State Delegation Page

additionalLocals.name: Daniel J. Hunt
additionalLocals.title: State Representative
committees: Committee on Environment and Natural Resources
Committee on Telecommunications, Utilities and Energy
additionalLocals.background: Daniel J. Hunt is the Massachusetts State Representative for the 13th Suffolk District, representing multiple Boston neighborhoods (Dorchester, Roslindale, West Roxbury) and a small portion of Quincy (Ward 3 Precinct 3). A Dorchester native and Boston College and Suffolk Law graduate, he has served since 2015 and is known for his work on environmental policy and constituent services.
additionalLocals.backgroundFull:
Daniel J. Hunt is the Massachusetts State Representative for the 13th Suffolk District. His district is centered in Boston, covering Ward 7 Precinct 9, Ward 13 Precincts 6–10, Ward 15 Precinct 6, Ward 16 Precincts 1–7, 9, 10, and 12, and Ward 17 Precincts 3 and 5 — as well as Quincy Ward 3 Precinct 3 in Norfolk County.

Note: Rep. Hunt represents only one precinct within Quincy (Ward 3 Precinct 3). The majority of Quincy is served by Tackey Chan (2nd Norfolk), Bruce J. Ayers (1st Norfolk), and Ronald Mariano (3rd Norfolk). Quincy residents should confirm their district at malegislature.gov.

Hunt was born and raised in Dorchester, graduating from Boston Latin Academy in 1999. He earned a B.S. in Business and Management Economics from Boston College's Carroll School of Management and a J.D. from Suffolk University Law School. Before his election he served as chief of staff for Representative Daniel Bosley (2008–2011), worked on the John Kerry 2004 presidential campaign, and served as Director of Governmental Affairs at the Massachusetts Department of Conservation and Recreation.

Hunt was elected to the State House in 2014, succeeding Marty Walsh who had just been elected Mayor of Boston. He sits on the Committee on Environment and Natural Resources and the Committee on Telecommunications, Utilities and Energy. He has focused his work on parks, open space, environmental justice, and economic development for his urban district.

Constituents in Quincy Ward 3 Precinct 3 can reach Rep. Hunt at Daniel.Hunt@mahouse.gov or (617) 722-2460.

sources.label: MA Legislature — Rep. Hunt Official Profile

additionalLocals.name: Noel T. DiBona
additionalLocals.title: Councillor-at-Large
additionalLocals.office: Historic City Hall, 1305 Hancock Street, Quincy, MA 02169
additionalLocals.background: Noel T. DiBona is a Councillor-at-Large on the Quincy City Council and the longest-serving current member. A lifelong Quincy resident, he ran the family landscaping business for 26 years, served on the School Committee from 2013 and on the council since 2016, and served as Council President in 2022 and 2023. He has focused on downtown revitalization, responsible development, traffic and parking, affordability, and schools.
additionalLocals.backgroundFull:
Noel DiBona is a lifelong Quincy resident who graduated from Quincy High School in 1993 before earning an Associate's degree from Quincy College and a Bachelor's degree in Sociology from UMass Boston. He and his wife Niamh are raising three children who all attend Quincy Public Schools.

He ran the family landscaping and snow removal business, Russ DiBona & Son, for over 26 years. He also worked for the Norfolk County Sheriff's Office — first helping seniors prevent scams and fraud, then coordinating re-entry programs for inmates — and later served as Director of Administrative Services at the Norfolk County Registry of Deeds. He currently works as an insurance consultant.

Noel began his public service career on the Quincy School Committee in 2013, then has served as a Councillor-at-Large since 2016 — the longest-serving current council member. He served as Council President in 2022 and 2023.

His stated priorities include downtown quality of life and the transformation of Quincy Center; responsible development opposing overdevelopment without adequate affordable housing and parking; addressing traffic congestion and parking citywide; ensuring developments include affordable and workforce housing; and school advocacy as a former School Committee member and QPS parent.

Note: This profile is informational. It does not represent an endorsement.

additionalLocals.name: Anne Mahoney
additionalLocals.title: Councillor-at-Large — Council President
additionalLocals.office: Historic City Hall, 1305 Hancock Street, Quincy, MA 02169
additionalLocals.background: Anne Mahoney is a Councillor-at-Large and Council President of the Quincy City Council. A lifelong Quincy resident, she earned a Bachelor's in Finance from Suffolk University and built a career in marketing, banking, and strategy. She served on the School Committee from 2006 to 2018 — overseeing a $90 million school budget — and as a Councillor-at-Large from 2018 to 2024 before being re-elected in 2025. She has focused on fiscal accountability, managing the city's $1.6 billion debt, balanced development, and restoring public trust.
additionalLocals.backgroundFull:
Anne Mahoney is a lifelong Quincy resident and Council President of the Quincy City Council. She graduated from Quincy High School, earned a Bachelor's degree in Finance from Suffolk University, and built a career in marketing, banking, and business strategy at Citizens Bank, Century Bank, Santander Bank, and several private firms. She co-founded Cyclone Design, Inc. and hosted a community podcast called City Limits.

Anne has served Quincy in elected office for over 18 years — first as a School Committee member from 2006 to 2018, where she helped oversee a $90 million school budget and chaired the Budget, Finance, and Special Education subcommittees — then as a Councillor-at-Large from 2018 to 2024. She was re-elected in 2025 and currently serves as Council President.

Her stated priorities include fiscal accountability and independent oversight; managing the city's $1.6 billion debt through regular public reporting and responsible borrowing; balanced development tied to a citywide master plan; restoring public trust by requiring public hearings on major decisions; and communication through her City Limits podcast, neighborhood listening sessions, and social media.

Note: This profile is informational. It does not represent an endorsement.

additionalLocals.name: Ziqiang Yuan
additionalLocals.title: Councillor-at-Large
additionalLocals.office: Historic City Hall, 1305 Hancock Street, Quincy, MA 02169
additionalLocals.background: Ziqiang "Susan" Yuan is a Councillor-at-Large on the Quincy City Council, first elected in 2025. Born and raised in China, she earned a Ph.D. in genetics and came to the US in 1999 for postdoctoral research at the University of Vermont. She is the only foreign-born candidate to have won elected office in Quincy in recent memory. She has focused on transparency, fiscal responsibility, fair pay, and civic engagement.
additionalLocals.backgroundFull:
Ziqiang "Susan" Yuan was born and raised in a working-class family in China, earning a Ph.D. in genetics before receiving a scholarship to come to the United States in 1999 for postdoctoral research at the University of Vermont. She met her husband Heiko, a German software entrepreneur, at a Taiji club in Boston's Chinatown. They live in Houghs Neck.

After eight years in academic research at UVM and Dartmouth, Susan built experience across diverse fields — cooking meals at a nonprofit, promoting Asian culture through publishing, early childhood education, public service, and most recently as a medical assistant at Action Medicine in Braintree. She became civically active after the council approved large pay raises for the mayor and themselves without public input, helping lead the campaign to oppose those raises. She is active in WeVoters, A Just Quincy, and the Quincy Badminton Club, and ran as an Independent — the only foreign-born candidate to win elected office in Quincy in recent memory.

Her stated priorities include transparency and public input on major decisions; stricter oversight of city finances, open bidding, regular audits, and public access to budget and contract data; fair pay for frontline city workers before top officials receive raises; proposing mayoral term limits; encouraging immigrant and first-time voters to participate; and opposing public spending on the $850,000 bronze statues at the public safety headquarters.

Note: This profile is informational. It does not represent an endorsement.

additionalLocals.name: David Jacobs
additionalLocals.title: Ward 1 Councillor
additionalLocals.office: Historic City Hall, 1305 Hancock Street, Quincy, MA 02169
additionalLocals.background: David Jacobs is the Ward 1 Councillor on the Quincy City Council, first elected in 2025. A lifelong Quincy resident and QPS graduate, he is a teacher with Boston Public Schools and former National Park Service Ranger. He lives in Ward 1 with his family and has focused on transparent government, community access, fiscal accountability, public housing, and schools.
additionalLocals.backgroundFull:
David Jacobs is a lifelong Quincy resident and proud product of Quincy Public Schools. He and his family live in Ward One — the neighborhood he represents — where his children attend Atherton Hough Elementary and preschool.

David has been a teacher with Boston Public Schools since 2012. He previously worked as a Park Ranger with the National Park Service at Adams National Historical Park, Badlands National Park, and Saugus Ironworks. He holds graduate certificates in Human Resources Management from Framingham State University and Special Education/Autism from Salem State College.

Before running for council, he served on the Houghs Neck Community Council for many years and was a longtime board member of the Quincy Band Boosters.

His stated priorities include transparent government where residents — not developers — come first; quarterly town halls, an online newsletter, and translation services; repealing the mayor's 79% raise and the council's 50% raise; ensuring public housing residents have stronger representation on the Quincy Housing Authority Board; and making sure school investments serve Ward One families.

Note: This profile is informational. It does not represent an endorsement.

additionalLocals.name: Richard C. Ash
additionalLocals.title: Ward 2 Councillor
additionalLocals.office: Historic City Hall, 1305 Hancock Street, Quincy, MA 02169
additionalLocals.background: Richard C. Ash is the Ward 2 Councillor on the Quincy City Council, first elected in 2025. A fifth-generation Quincy resident and Quincy Point native, he holds a B.S. in Finance and a law degree from Suffolk University and practices civil litigation, family law, and election law. He serves as President of the Ward 2 Civic Association and Quincy Pride, Inc., and is a Chamber of Commerce Ambassador.
additionalLocals.backgroundFull:
Richard C. Ash is a fifth-generation Quincy resident and third-generation Quincy Point native. His great-grandfather worked at the Fore River Shipyard. He lives in a multi-family home in Quincy Point with his partner Mateus, his mother Abby, and his Aunt Tina.

Richard attended Quincy Public Schools before earning a Bachelor of Science in Finance (cum laude) and his law degree from Suffolk University. He practices civil litigation, family law, and election law at Baker, Braverman & Barbadoro, P.C. in Quincy. He serves as President of both the Ward 2 Civic Association and Quincy Pride, Inc., is an Ambassador for the Quincy Chamber of Commerce, and was named a Quincy Dems COVID Hero in 2021 for helping seniors with groceries and prescriptions during the pandemic. He is also a Justice of the Peace and Notary Public.

His stated priorities include senior safety and security, particularly at 1000 Southern Artery and the Fore River Clubhouse; smart development protecting green spaces, beaches, and the neighborhood character of Quincy Point and Penn's Hill; affordable housing for multi-generational Quincy families; supporting businesses along the Washington Street Corridor; communication and transparency through quarterly community meetings; and celebrating the diversity of Ward 2.

Note: This profile is informational. It does not represent an endorsement.

additionalLocals.name: Walter Hubley
additionalLocals.title: Ward 3 Councillor
additionalLocals.office: Historic City Hall, 1305 Hancock Street, Quincy, MA 02169
additionalLocals.background: Walter Hubley is the Ward 3 Councillor on the Quincy City Council, first elected in 2025. A Quincy native and North Quincy High graduate, he has led the Wollaston Hill Neighborhood Association for 15 years and created PorchFest Quincy. He was VP of Strategic Technology at Atlantic Broadband, CEO at the Woodward School for Girls, and is currently Senior IT Program Manager at The Community Builders, one of the country's leading nonprofit affordable housing organizations.
additionalLocals.backgroundFull:
Walter Hubley grew up on Wollaston Hill and still lives in his family home there with his wife Kathryn, a Quincy School Committee member. He is the adopted son of the late Edgar and Ruth Hubley, who fostered nearly 40 children over 30 years. Walter and Kathryn became foster parents during COVID-19 and later hosted international students from Hong Kong, China, Vietnam, and Costa Rica.

Walter attended Quincy Public Schools — Wollaston Elementary, Central Middle School, and North Quincy High (Class of 1989). He spent over a decade as Vice President of Strategic Technology at Atlantic Broadband, then served as CEO and Head of School at the Woodward School for Girls from 2016 to 2018. He currently works as a Senior IT Program Manager at The Community Builders, one of the country's leading nonprofit affordable housing organizations.

In the community, Walter co-founded the Wollaston Hill Neighborhood Association — leading it for 15 years — created PorchFest Quincy, and secured a $144,000 Community Preservation Act grant to restore Safford Park, which reopened in 2015.

His stated priorities include Ward 3 residents first; government transparency and accountability; responsible development that respects neighborhood history and green spaces (including opposing a proposed Taco Bell drive-thru at the Newport Avenue Stop & Shop); and community building through events like PorchFest and neighborhood cookouts.

Note: This profile is informational. It does not represent an endorsement.

additionalLocals.name: Virginia Ryan
additionalLocals.title: Ward 4 Councillor
additionalLocals.office: Historic City Hall, 1305 Hancock Street, Quincy, MA 02169
additionalLocals.background: Virginia Ryan is the Ward 4 Councillor on the Quincy City Council, first elected in 2025. Originally from Dorchester, she has lived in Quincy for over 20 years. She spent 25 years in accounting and benefits management at Gillette and Procter & Gamble. Active in WeVoters, Quincy Votes, and AJust Quincy, she has focused on transparency, community access, fiscal responsibility, and voter engagement.
additionalLocals.backgroundFull:
Virginia Ryan has lived in Quincy for over 20 years, where she raised her two children. Originally from Dorchester, she attended public schools before studying Accounting at Quincy College, where she also volunteered as a literacy tutor.

Virginia spent 25 years in accounting and benefits management at Gillette and Procter & Gamble, overseeing retirement plans, payroll, and employee benefits for thousands of workers. She later worked in healthcare administration and real estate.

She became more politically active after the 2016 national election and was inspired to run for local office after witnessing very low voter turnout in a 2023 Quincy special election. She is active in WeVoters, Quincy Votes, and AJust Quincy — three groups focused on increasing resident voices and voter registration.

Her stated priorities include transparency and accountability in city government; quarterly town halls and public comment before council meetings; repealing the mayor's 79% raise, the council's 50% raise, and automatic annual raises; and civic engagement through her work in voter registration.

Note: This profile is informational. It does not represent an endorsement.

additionalLocals.name: Maggie McKee
additionalLocals.title: Ward 5 Councillor
additionalLocals.office: Historic City Hall, 1305 Hancock Street, Quincy, MA 02169
additionalLocals.background: Maggie McKee is the Ward 5 Councillor on the Quincy City Council, first elected in 2025. She studied philosophy and physics at Grinnell College, earned a graduate certificate in science writing from UC Santa Cruz, and worked as an award-winning science journalist. She co-founded Quincy Votes! in 2021 and the Quincy Tree Alliance, and in 2024 helped organize the 6,000-signature campaign against the mayor's and council's pay raises. She has been endorsed by the Environmental League of Massachusetts Action Fund.
additionalLocals.backgroundFull:
Maggie McKee was born in Washington, DC, and raised by her late mother Kitty. She studied philosophy and physics at Grinnell College, taught English in Vietnam and Japan, and earned a graduate certificate in science writing from UC Santa Cruz. She worked as a science journalist covering climate change and space before moving to Wollaston with her husband in 2009. She has lived in Quincy for 15 years and is raising her son here.

Maggie served on the board of the Quincy Climate Action Network, chaired a city task force on composting, and founded the Quincy Tree Alliance to protect trees that cool the air and prevent flooding. In 2021 she co-founded Quincy Votes! to fight low voter turnout. In 2024, she helped organize a campaign that gathered 6,000 signatures in 20 days against the mayor's 79% and the council's 50% raises — forcing politicians to delay them.

She has been endorsed by the Environmental League of Massachusetts Action Fund, 350 Mass Action, and named a 'rising star' by Mass Alliance.

Her stated priorities include government transparency with monthly public hearings and conflict-of-interest disclosure; quarterly town halls and translation services; repealing the mayor's and council's raises; protecting Quincy's tree canopy, composting, and clean energy; affordable housing serving everyday residents; and civic engagement through Quincy Votes!

Note: This profile is informational. It does not represent an endorsement.

additionalLocals.name: Deborah Riley
additionalLocals.title: Ward 6 Councillor
additionalLocals.office: Historic City Hall, 1305 Hancock Street, Quincy, MA 02169
additionalLocals.background: Deborah Riley is the Ward 6 Councillor on the Quincy City Council, first elected in 2025 after previously running in 2023. A UMass Lowell graduate in Information Systems, she spent 10 years as a quality assurance engineer at Fidelity Investments before becoming a licensed real estate agent. She has volunteered extensively with the Squantum School PTO, Quincy Lions, Rotary, and youth hockey, and founded the Promposal college financial literacy program.
additionalLocals.backgroundFull:
Deborah Riley was born in Billerica and has lived in Quincy for over 35 years, raising her two sons here — one of whom is a Quincy police officer. She is a UMass Lowell graduate with a bachelor's degree in Information Systems and spent 10 years as a quality assurance engineer at Fidelity Investments before becoming a licensed real estate agent. She is a certified yoga instructor and founded the 'Promposal' college financial literacy program.

She has volunteered extensively — serving as President and Treasurer of the Squantum School PTO, as well as with Star of the Sea Church, St. Ann's Church, Quincy Youth Hockey, and the Quincy Lions and Rotary Clubs. She ran for Ward 6 in 2023 before winning the seat in 2025.

Her stated priorities include communication and transparency with mailed newsletters and resident meetings; fiscal oversight and auditing, including of the Community Preservation Fund and the bronze statues at the public safety headquarters; housing including property tax credits for owner-occupied homes and incentives for 55+ housing; expanding the seasonal Squantum ferry and road/transit project updates; reviewing the Granite Links Golf Course lease extension with an independent real estate specialist; protecting Quincy's 27 miles of beaches; and advocating for Long Island Bridge alternatives for mental health access.

representative.title: U.S. Representative
representative.office: 2369 Rayburn House Office Building, Washington, DC 20515
representative.background: Stephen Lynch represents Massachusetts' 8th Congressional District, covering parts of Boston, the South Shore, and communities including Sharon. A former ironworker and union president, Lynch was first elected in 2001 following the death of Rep. Joe Moakley.
representative.backgroundFull:
Stephen Lynch represents Massachusetts' 8th Congressional District, which includes communities on Boston's South Shore including Sharon, Stoughton, Canton, Brockton, and portions of Boston. He was first elected in 2001 in a special election following the death of longtime Congressman Joe Moakley.

Lynch grew up in South Boston's Old Colony Housing Project, one of seven children. He became an ironworker and union president before earning his law degree from Boston College Law School. His working-class roots shape his legislative priorities: he has been a consistent advocate for union workers, veterans, and infrastructure investment.

On the House Financial Services Committee, Lynch has been a leading voice on cryptocurrency regulation and postal banking reform. On the Oversight Committee, he has investigated prescription drug pricing and government contracting. He is known as a moderate-to-conservative Democrat on some social issues while remaining reliably progressive on labor and economic policy.

For Sharon residents, Rep. Lynch's district office is located in Boston and his staff can assist with federal agency matters including Social Security, Veterans Affairs, USCIS, and passport services.

representative.name: Edward R. Philips
representative.title: State Representative
representative.background: Edward R. 'Ted' Philips is the Massachusetts State Representative for the 8th Norfolk District, representing Sharon, Stoughton, Walpole, and Mansfield. Born in Sharon, he is a lifelong resident who served as a State House staffer for 15 years before winning election in 2020.
representative.backgroundFull:
Edward R. 'Ted' Philips is the Massachusetts State Representative for the 8th Norfolk District. He was born and raised in Sharon, making him one of the few members of the legislature to represent the town where he grew up.

Philips graduated from the University of Massachusetts Amherst (B.A., 2005) and earned a Master of Public Administration from Suffolk University (2011). Before running for office, he spent 15 years as a professional legislative staffer on Beacon Hill, developing deep expertise in the legislative process and building relationships across both parties.

He won the Democratic primary in September 2020 and was elected to the House in November 2020 to represent Sharon, Stoughton, Walpole, and Mansfield. He has been re-elected in 2022 and 2024.

In the House, Philips serves on the Committee on Environment and Natural Resources and the Committee on Housing. His legislative work has included bills related to Sharon's water quality, local governance reforms (including the expansion of the Sharon Select Board from three to five members), and environmental justice. He previously served on the Sharon Finance Committee and the Sharon Democratic Town Committee.

For Sharon residents, Rep. Philips is easily accessible via phone and email. His Beacon Hill office provides constituent services for state agency matters including the Registry of Motor Vehicles, MassHealth, Department of Unemployment Assistance, and state permit processes.

senator.title: State Senator

Feeney is the Massachusetts State Senator for the Bristol and Norfolk District, representing Sharon along with Attleboro, Foxborough, Mansfield, Norton, and surrounding communities. First elected in a 2017 special election, Feeney is a labor attorney and former union organizer.
senator.backgroundFull:
Paul R. Feeney represents the Bristol and Norfolk District in the Massachusetts State Senate, a district covering Sharon, Attleboro, Foxborough, Mansfield, Norton, Plainville, and Walpole.

Feeney won his seat in a competitive 2017 special election, defeating Republican Jacob Ventura. He is a labor attorney by training and was previously an organizer and advocate for working families in the region. He has been re-elected continuously since.

In the Senate, Feeney focuses on labor and workforce development, economic development, and environmental protection. He has partnered with Rep. Edward Philips on several Sharon-specific legislative initiatives, including bills related to the town's water quality and local governance.

For Sharon residents, Sen. Feeney holds regular office hours in the district and his staff assists with state agency matters including unemployment, MassHealth, the Registry of Motor Vehicles, and state permit processes.

additionalLocals.title: Select Board Member — Chair

She is an attorney and Associate Dean at Northeastern University School of Law, president of the Sharon Racial Equity Alliance, and was named Sharon's Citizen of the Year in 2021.
additionalLocals.backgroundFull:
Kiana Baskin (née Pierre-Louis), Esq. is the Chair of the Sharon Select Board, serving since 2022. She made history as the first Black person elected to the Select Board in Sharon.

Professionally, Baskin is an Associate Dean for Equity, Belonging & Student Affairs and assistant teaching professor at Northeastern University School of Law. Earlier in her career, she was a Partner and Owner at Perkins & Baskin, P.C., where she specialized in real estate law, corporate law, and civil litigation. She later transitioned to academia, teaching business law and DEI-focused courses at Bentley University before joining Northeastern.

In Sharon, she previously chaired the town's Diversity, Equity and Inclusion Committee and is President and board member of the Sharon Racial Equity Alliance (SREA). She has been appointed by the Governor to the Metropolitan Area Planning Council (MAPC) and serves on the MBTA Advisory Board. She is also a member of the Boston Bar Association's DEI Steering Committee.

Baskin was honored as Sharon's Citizen of the Year in 2021. In 2026, she was elected Vice President of the Massachusetts Municipal Association (MMA).

On the Select Board, she serves on the Priorities Committee and acts as the Board's liaison to the Diversity, Equity & Inclusion Committee.

additionalLocals.backgroundFull:
Hanna Switlekowski is Vice Chair of the Sharon Select Board, first elected in 2023, and is the youngest person ever elected to the Board in Sharon's history. She is a lifelong Sharon resident and Sharon High School graduate (Class of 2009), where she received the Ordinary Hero Award in 2008.

She holds a Bachelor of Arts in Near Eastern Judaic Studies & Politics from Brandeis University (2013), where she also serves on the Alumni Board as Greater Boston President, and a Master of Public Administration (MPA) from Northeastern University School of Public Policy and Urban Affairs.

Professionally, Switlekowski serves as Deputy Director of the MBTA Advisory Board, where she focuses on transit accountability and reliability. She brings years of Beacon Hill experience: she previously served as Legislative Director for State Senator Paul Feeney, and as a Legislative Aide for former State Representative Louis Kafka.

On the Select Board, she serves on the Capital Outlay Committee and acts as liaison to the Commission on Disabilities. She has been active in pursuing accessibility grants (including brick sidewalks and door-opener improvements) and participating in the Sharon Food Pantry Food Drive.

Xander Shapiro was elected to the Sharon Select Board in May 2024 to a three-year term, and serves as Clerk of the Board. He describes himself as a 'community trustee' committed to listening to residents and delivering results.

Professionally, Shapiro is a General Manager and business strategy executive with decades of experience in consumer products companies, startups, and nonprofit organizations. He is founder of Akorn Technology Inc., an ag-tech company focused on natural post-harvest produce solutions.

Before joining the Select Board, he served as Vice Chair of Sharon's Planning Board, as a member of the Sharon Housing Authority, and on the Municipal Solar Oversight Committee — where he helped spearhead solar projects that have saved the town approximately $750,000 annually and generated over $12 million in cumulative revenue and savings. He holds a Ph.D. in Civil & Environmental Engineering from MIT and an MBA from Boston University. He served 11 years on the Sharon School Committee (including 3 years as Chair) and has been a delegate to two Democratic National Conventions.
additionalLocals.backgroundFull:
Sam Liao was elected to the Sharon Select Board in 2025 and has been a Sharon resident for over 30 years. He and his family moved to Sharon, where his children attended the public schools. He describes Sharon as 'my heaven on earth.'

Liao holds B.S. and M.S. degrees in Civil & Environmental Engineering from Cornell University, a Ph.D. from MIT, and an MBA from Boston University. He has worked as a civil and environmental engineer for his professional career and was recognized by the American Society of Civil Engineers in 2008.

His civic record in Sharon spans decades: he served on the Sharon High School Expansion Building Committee (1995–1999), then on the Sharon School Committee for eleven years (1999–2010), including three years as Chair, playing a role in the renovation and expansion of four of Sharon's five school buildings and installing the first solar panels in Sharon atop the Sharon Middle School. He served on the Town Charter Commission (2007–2009), the Capital Outlay Committee, the Priorities Committee, the Personnel Board, and the Transportation Advisory Board. He was also elected a delegate to the 2008 and 2012 Democratic National Conventions.

As an environmental engineer, he proposed installing a solar farm on the Mountain Street landfill over 20 years ago — a project now coming to fruition. On the Select Board, he serves as liaison to the Lake Massapoag Advisory Committee, where his civil engineering background is particularly relevant to the town's water quality initiatives.

president.name: Donald J. Trump
president.title: President of the United States
president.background: 47th President of the United States, serving his second term beginning January 20, 2025. Former businessman and television personality. 45th President 2017–2021.
president.backgroundFull:
Donald J. Trump is the 47th President of the United States, having been inaugurated on January 20, 2025, following his victory in the November 2024 presidential election over Democratic candidate Kamala Harris. He previously served as the 45th President from January 20, 2017 to January 20, 2021.

Trump was born on June 14, 1946, in Queens, New York City, the fourth of five children of Fred Trump, a real estate developer, and Mary Anne MacLeod Trump. He attended the New York Military Academy and transferred to the Wharton School of the University of Pennsylvania, graduating in 1968 with a degree in economics.

Before entering politics, Trump built a career in real estate and entertainment. He expanded his father's real estate company into Manhattan, developed properties including Trump Tower (1983) and a range of hotels and golf courses internationally, and hosted the NBC reality television series The Apprentice from 2004 to 2015. He filed for corporate bankruptcy six times between 1991 and 2009 relating to his casino and hotel businesses in Atlantic City, New Jersey.

Trump announced his first presidential campaign in June 2015 and won the Republican nomination before defeating Democratic nominee Hillary Clinton in the November 2016 general election. His first term was marked by major tax legislation (Tax Cuts and Jobs Act, 2017), the appointment of three Supreme Court justices, renegotiation of the USMCA trade agreement, and the COVID-19 pandemic response. He was impeached twice by the House of Representatives — in December 2019 for abuse of power and obstruction of Congress, and in January 2021 for incitement of insurrection following the January 6 Capitol riot — and was acquitted by the Senate both times.

Following his 2020 defeat to Joe Biden, Trump announced a 2024 campaign in November 2022 and secured the Republican nomination despite facing multiple criminal indictments. He was convicted in May 2024 by a New York jury on 34 felony counts of falsifying business records, becoming the first former U.S. president to be convicted of a felony. He was sentenced in January 2025 to an unconditional discharge.

In the 2024 election, Trump defeated Vice President Kamala Harris, winning 312 Electoral College votes to Harris's 226 and becoming the first president since Grover Cleveland to serve two non-consecutive terms. His second term has focused on immigration enforcement, tariff imposition, federal workforce reduction, and withdrawal from international agreements.

For Massachusetts residents, the President leads the executive branch and signs or vetoes all federal legislation. Contact the White House at 202-456-1111 or visit whitehouse.gov.

sources.label: White House — President Trump Official Bio

vicePresident.name: JD Vance
vicePresident.title: Vice President of the United States
vicePresident.office: Eisenhower Executive Office Building, Washington, DC 20501
vicePresident.background: Vice President of the United States since January 2025. Former U.S. Senator from Ohio (2023–2025). Author of 'Hillbilly Elegy.' Yale Law School graduate.
vicePresident.backgroundFull:
JD Vance is the 50th Vice President of the United States, having been inaugurated on January 20, 2025, alongside President Donald Trump. He previously served as U.S. Senator from Ohio from January 2023 to January 2025, and before that was best known as the author of the 2016 memoir Hillbilly Elegy.

Vance was born James Donald Bowman on August 2, 1984, in Middletown, Ohio — a Rust Belt city he later documented in his memoir. Raised partly by his maternal grandparents (his "Mamaw and Papaw") amid family instability and his mother's struggles with opioid addiction, he graduated from Middletown High School and enlisted in the U.S. Marine Corps, serving in Iraq as a public affairs officer from 2003 to 2007. He then earned a B.A. summa cum laude in political science and philosophy from Ohio State University (2009) and a J.D. from Yale Law School (2013), where he met his wife Usha Chilukuri.

After Yale, Vance worked as a lawyer and venture capitalist in San Francisco and Cincinnati, backed by PayPal co-founder Peter Thiel. His 2016 memoir Hillbilly Elegy: A Memoir of a Family and Culture in Crisis became a New York Times bestseller and was adapted into a Netflix film in 2020, bringing him national attention as a voice on white working-class culture in Appalachia and the Midwest.

Vance ran for the Republican Senate nomination in Ohio in 2022, reversing his earlier criticism of Donald Trump to earn Trump's endorsement, and won the general election over Democratic incumbent Tim Ryan. In the Senate he served on the Banking Committee and was known as an ardent Trump loyalist and opponent of military aid to Ukraine. Trump selected him as his running mate in July 2024 at the Republican National Convention.

As Vice President, Vance presides over the U.S. Senate and casts tie-breaking votes. He has been an active advocate for the Trump administration's domestic and foreign policy agenda and is widely considered a leading contender for the Republican presidential nomination in 2028.

For Massachusetts residents, the Vice President's office can be contacted at 202-456-1414 or through whitehouse.gov.
